schools.summary: Matthew (the ‘face of a man’) opens with Christ’s human genealogy; Isaiah’s ‘who shall declare his generation?’ (Isa 53:8) speaks of the divine; Ambrose: David and Abraham the two heads of the line.

Against the heretics who made Christ a mere man (Ebion, Paul of Samosata), the eternal deity is set — ‘In the beginning was the Word’ (Jn 1:1), ‘in the form of God’ (Phil 2:6); Cyril of Jerusalem: Son of David at the end of ages, Son of God before all ages.
‘The book of the generation’ (biblos geneseōs), a Hebrew title for a narrative of descent; the carefully kept Jewish genealogies (Lightfoot).
Calvin opens the debate whether both Evangelists trace through Joseph, or Matthew through Joseph and Luke through Mary.

debates.question: Do Matthew and Luke both trace through Joseph, or Matthew through Joseph and Luke through Mary?

sides.position: Matthew through Joseph, Luke through Mary

themes: son of David, son of Abraham
against the heresies of a mere-man Christ

schools.summary: The line of promise traced (‘begat,’ egennēsen, = direct descent); Ishmael and Esau omitted as outside the covenant (Gen 21:12), yet ‘Judah and his brethren’ all named as the people of God.
Ambrose: Abraham the first witness of faith (Gen 15:6); Pseudo-Chrysostom finds Christ figured in the names — Isaac ‘laughter,’ Judah ‘confessor’ (Mt 11:25).
Calvin: the twelve patriarchs all given a place, all sharing the favour of adoption — the descent marked by sovereign election (Isaac not Ishmael, Jacob not Esau).

Jerome: the genealogy names not holy women but such as Scripture condemned (Tamar), that ‘He who came for sinners, born of sinners, might put away the sins of all’; Ambrose contrasts Luke’s ‘immaculate’ priestly line.
The history of the line — Judah begat Phares and Zarah of Tamar before Egypt, on through Esrom and Aram (Gen 38:29; Ruth 4:18).

The line through the Exodus generation: Naasson (Nahshon), prince of Judah at the Exodus and brother-in-law of Aaron (Num 1:7; 1 Chr 2:10); the succession agreeing exactly with the OT records (Ruth 4:19-20).

Matthew adds what the OT omits — that Boaz’s mother was Rahab the harlot of Jericho (Josh 6:25; Ruth 4:21) — and names Ruth the Moabitess (Ruth 4:13); two Gentile women gathered into the line by faith.

themes: Gentiles and sinners in the line

schools.summary: Bathsheba is unnamed (called ‘wife of Urias’) to recall the great sin (Remigius, Gloss); Ambrose praises David’s repentance; Augustine: the descent through Solomon fits Christ’s taking on our sins.
Calvin/Wesley: ‘the king’ is given to David alone, the type of the Messiah whose throne Christ inherits (Lk 1:32); Gill: the despised youngest son chosen by God.
The line from Solomon’s glory into the divided kingdom: Roboam (born of Naamah the Ammonitess, 1 Kgs 14:21), Abia, Asa (1 Chr 3:10).
Pseudo-Chrysostom reads the names mystically — Solomon ‘the peacemaker,’ Roboam ‘by a multitude of people.’

themes: the throne kept for the Messiah

schools.summary: Three kings (Ahaziah, Joash, Amaziah) omitted between Joram and Ozias (1 Chr 3:11-12) — ‘begat’ meaning mediate descent; the omission serving the three-fourteens structure (v.17).
Augustine: the three excluded for continuous wickedness; Hilary/Pseudo-Chrysostom: descendants of Ahab through Athaliah, cut off under the curse to the fourth generation — the Gentile stain purged.

debates.question: Why are the three kings omitted?

sides.position: divine judgment — descendants of Ahab under the curse / continuous wickedness

schools.summary: Ozias = Uzziah (leprous for presumption, 2 Chr 26:16); three kings omitted before him to compress into three fourteens (v.17); the line runs good king Jotham, wicked Ahaz, godly Hezekiah.
The descent through Manasseh (born of Hephzibah, 2 Kgs 21:1) — ‘remarkable both for his sins and his humiliation’ (2 Chr 33:12-13) — through Amon to the reformer Josiah.

themes: grace to the worst of sinners

schools.summary: Difficulties: Josiah was Jechonias’ grandfather (Jehoiakim omitted, 2 Kgs 23:34; 1 Chr 3:15-16); ‘his brethren’ = kinsmen/uncles; the verse marks the deportation to Babylon, the line’s lowest point yet unbroken.

The line resumes after Babylon (Jechonias → Salathiel → Zorobabel, 1 Chr 3:17); Jeremiah’s ‘write this man childless’ (Jer 22:30) meant no descendant should prosper on David’s earthly throne, not absolute childlessness.
Ambrose raises the Jer 22:30 problem; Remigius and Rabanus read the post-exilic names as mystical figures of Christ.
Calvin: the captivity turned David’s royal descendants into exiles and slaves, yet was arranged by providence to preserve the seed.

debates.question: How is Christ of Jechonias’ seed if Coniah was ‘written childless’ (Jer 22:30)?

sides.position: the prophecy meant no descendant prospering on David’s earthly throne, not childlessness

schools.summary: The line passes into post-exilic generations unrecorded in the OT (Abiud not in Chronicles, perhaps = Meshullam, 1 Chr 3:19), resting on the carefully kept Jewish registers.
Finding little history, the fathers read the names mystically — Remigius: Abiud = ‘He is my Father’ (Jn 10:30); Rabanus traces a moral sense pointing to Christ.
The succession continues through Azor, Sadoc, Achim, Eliud — names belonging to the obscure inter-testamental period, recorded in the Evangelist’s source but nowhere else in Scripture.
The fathers read the very names as hidden foreshadowings of Christ, after Rabanus and Remigius on the surrounding verses.
The last generations before Joseph (Eleazar, Matthan, Jacob), names not in the OT; JFB reaffirms the agreement with 1 Chr 3:17 and that Jer 22:30 is no contradiction.

The formula breaks: not ‘Joseph begat Jesus’ but ‘Joseph the husband of Mary, of whom (feminine) was born Jesus’ — guarding the virgin birth; the Joseph son-of-Jacob/son-of-Heli difference explained by nature/law, levirate (Eusebius), or Mary’s line (Lightfoot, Wesley).
Joseph the legal/adoptive father (Augustine); ‘who is called Christ’ = anointed by the Spirit (Hilary); against Ebion (Gennadius), Nestorius (Ephesus), and dividing the one Christ (Pseudo-Augustine).

debates.question: Why does Matthew call Joseph son of Jacob, and Luke son of Heli?

sides.position: father by nature vs. by law
Matthew traces Joseph’s line, Luke Mary’s

themes: Son of David by law, Son of God by nature

schools.summary: The three groups of fourteen are a memoria technica (common among the Jews), of unequal length, with omitted names and an overlap (David ends/begins, Jesus ends) to make the count even.
Chrysostom: the three divisions (patriarchs/judges, kings, post-exile) show that no change of government made the people better.
Remigius: fourteen = ten (Decalogue) + four (Gospels), Law and Gospel agreeing; Augustine: Jechonias counted twice at the captivity’s turning-point.
The chain of begettings breaks for a unique birth; ‘espoused’ = a binding betrothal (husband/wife, not yet come together); the child conceived ‘of the Holy Ghost,’ not by man.
Origen and Cyril defend Mary as ‘mother of God’ against Nestorius — she bears the One who is God, not merely ‘the Christ.’

Jerome: conceived of a virgin espoused that her family be known, she be shielded from the adultery-charge, and have a protector; Augustine: a wedlock without carnal desire, befitting the sinless One; Henry: adore the mystery.
‘Just’ (dikaios, upright) Joseph would neither marry one supposed unfaithful nor expose her, but ‘put her away privily’ — the meeting of justice and mercy.
Jerome/Augustine/Ambrose: to spare her was the higher righteousness — guiltless of her blood, yet not betraying her.
Origen: Joseph suspected no sin but, seeing a ‘great sacrament’ in her, withdrew in reverent awe, thinking himself unworthy.

debates.question: Why did the ‘just’ Joseph seek to put Mary away?

sides.position: believing her unfaithful, he chose the gentlest course mercy allowed
he suspected no sin but withdrew in awe before the mystery

schools.summary: At the point of Joseph’s perplexity the angel appears in a dream, addressing him ‘son of David’ (recalling the promise) and ‘fear not… for that which is conceived is of the Holy Ghost.’
Augustine guards against making the Spirit the ‘father’ of Christ’s humanity (the Spirit is the agent, not father); Ambrose: calling Mary ‘wife’ does not deny her virginity.
Calvin: how seasonably the Lord aids his people — when he seems not to observe our cares, we are still under his eye.

themes: ‘Joseph, thou son of David, fear not’

schools.summary: ‘Jesus’ = Joshua, ‘he shall save’ — the name’s etymology given in ‘he shall save his people from their sins’; ‘call his name’ (not ‘give’), for the name was his from eternity.
Remigius: he saves his people (believers) from their sins, not from visible enemies; Chrysologus: ‘it is God that forgives sins,’ so the name proclaims him God.

The Evangelist’s comment that the birth fulfils prophecy (Isa 7:14, to Ahaz ~740 BC); his custom of confirming from the OT for Jewish believers; the ‘that it might be fulfilled’ (hina plērōthē) marking a correspondence no accident.
Chrysostom: the saying comprises the depths of mercy — the laws of nature broken, He who was above all made lower than all; Calvin: it includes all that is divine in Christ, not the bare name.

Isa 7:14 refers to Mary and her son, of no other; the ‘sign’ must be ‘new and wonderful’ — a true virgin’s conception, not a young woman’s ordinary birth (Jerome); ‘they shall call’ = he shall really be.
Calvin: ‘Emmanuel, God with us’ teaches God present not merely by power but in the incarnate Son dwelling in our flesh; Theodotus: against Photinus, the child is God, not mere man.

debates.question: Does Isaiah’s ‘almah’ mean a true virgin?

sides.position: yes — only a virgin’s conception is a sign ‘new and wonderful’

schools.summary: Joseph promptly obeys, ‘taking his wife home’ (parelaben) — the formal completion of the marriage; his ready performance proves both his faith and his obedience (Calvin).
The Gloss: he did what, and as, the angel commanded — break off all delays; Remigius: by Joseph’s obedience we begin to be recalled, as by Adam’s disobedience we were ruined.
‘Knew her not’ (euphemism, imperfect) = continence till Jesus’ birth, affirming the virgin birth; ‘firstborn’ is absent from the best MSS; ‘till’ need not imply union afterward (cf. 2 Sam 6:23).
Jerome, against Helvidius, defends Mary’s perpetual virginity — ‘till’ and ‘firstborn’ do not imply other children.
Barnes/Clarke/Calvin/Wesley: the virgin birth is affirmed, but Scripture neither affirms nor denies later children — the text leaves the question open.

debates.question: Does ‘till… firstborn’ mean Mary had other children?

sides.position: no — Mary remained ever-virgin (the phrasing implies nothing after)
the text leaves it open; she may have had children later
